seo.title: Orlando Travel Guide - Where to Stay, Airports & Events
seo.description: Plan a Orlando, Florida trip with neighborhoods, airports, travel tips, events and hotel planning links.
seo.twitterDescription: Plan a Orlando, Florida trip with neighborhoods, airports, travel tips, events and hotel planning links.
seo.webpageDescription: Plan a Orlando, Florida trip with neighborhoods, airports, travel tips, events and hotel planning links.

heroText: Orlando is a Central Florida city in Orange County, known for a service economy built around tourism, conventions, entertainment and regional growth.  Beyond the theme parks, the city has a downtown core around Lake Eola, nearby historic Winter Park, major airport access and a large metro area that shapes Central Florida.

highlights.title: Walt Disney World Resort
highlights.text: Use Walt Disney World as a full-day planning anchor: hotel area, early entry, park transfers and evening returns can change the best Orlando base.

highlights.title: Universal Orlando Resort
highlights.text: Universal works best when you compare park days with International Drive, airport timing, dining plans and whether you need a car.

highlights.title: International Drive
highlights.text: International Drive is a practical hotel corridor for first visits, restaurants, attractions, shuttles and easier comparison shopping between Orlando bases.

highlights.title: Lake Eola Park
highlights.text: Lake Eola gives Downtown Orlando a lighter local stop, useful when you want a break from theme-park schedules and resort transfers.

highlights.title: Winter Park
highlights.text: Winter Park adds museums, lakes, restaurants and a calmer local base when the trip needs more than the major theme parks.

highlights.title: Kennedy Space Center day trip
highlights.text: Kennedy Space Center is not in Orlando, but it is a common day-trip decision that can affect rental car and hotel planning.

history.label: Origins
history.text: Fort Gatlin, as the Orlando area was once known, was established at what is now just south of the city limits by the 4th U.S.
The site of construction for Fort Gatlin, a defensible position with fresh water between three small lakes, was likely chosen because the location was on a main trail and is less than 250 yards.

This property is still owned by the Ward family, and can be seen from southbound flights out of Orlando International Airport immediately on the south side of SR 417. Post-Industrial Revolution Orlando became a.

history.label: 19th century
history.text: Also, just south of the Orlando International Airport in the Boggy Creek area are 150 acres (0.61 km2) of property homesteaded in the late 19th century by the Ward family.
Fanning on November 9, 1838, during the construction of a series of fortified encampments across Florida during the Second Seminole War.
When the U.S. military abandoned the fort in 1839, the surrounding community was built up by settlers.
Fort Gatlin was briefly reoccupied by the military for a few weeks during October and November 1849, and subsequently a volunteer militia was left to defend the settlement.
In 1857, the post office was removed from Jernigan, and opened under the name of Orlando at a new location in present-day downtown Orlando.
For a short time in 1879, the town revoked its charter, and was subsequently reincorporated.
Orlando AAB and Pinecastle AAF were transferred to the United States Air Force in 1947 when it became a separate service and were redesignated as air force bases (AFB).

history.label: 1960s
history.text: In the 1960s, the base subsequently became home to the 306th Bombardment Wing of the Strategic Air Command, operating B-52 Stratofortress and KC-135 Stratotanker aircraft, in addition to detachment operations by EC-121 and U-2.

A major factor in Orlando's growth occurred in 1962, when the new Orlando Jetport, the precursor of the present-day Orlando International Airport, was built from a portion of the McCoy Air Force Base.
McCoy Air Force Base officially closed in 1975, and most of it is now part of the airport.

When McCoy AFB closed in 1976, its runways and territory to its south and east were transferred to the city to become Orlando International Airport, while a small portion to the northwest was transferred.
